coredata.dc:title: A bionic strategy for addressing scale-span issues in all-carbon electrocatalytic systems
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuan
Guo, Yanting
Li, Minjie
Wang, Liang
Geng, Bijiang
Chen, Zhiwen
Li, Zhen
Pan, Dengyu
coredata.dc:description: We report the rational design of an all-carbon and all-scale electrocatalytic system with ultrahigh electrocatalytic activity outperforming Pt-based electrodes by quantum mechanics calculations and bionics. According to the calculations, N and O co-doped graphene quantum dots (N-O-GQDs) could offer an ultrahigh atomic ratio of high-activity edge defects (N-C+-O, O-C+, and N-C+) owing to the most marked edge effect and co-doping effect. To fully activate these atomic-scale defects for catalyzing the two-electron triiodide reduction applied in dye-sensitized solar cells, a mesh-capillary carbon matrix composed of carbon fibers and carbon nanotubes was constructed to well disperse the catalyst and provide fast pathways for both electron transport and electrolyte penetration/diffusion by imitating the sophisticated structure of the blood vessel system. The all-carbon ternary system exhibits high reduction potential (i.e. low overpotential) and low charge-transfer resistance, and delivers high open-circuit voltage of 0.81V, high short circuit current density of 15.77mAcm−2, and high conversion efficiency of 7.68%. Our study helps understand and address general and challenging scale-span issues in electrode reactions for developing more efficient and practical electrocatalytic systems merely by assembling facilely derived carbon materials as the catalyst, matrix or current collector without using other materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bionics
Carbon fibers
Carbon nanotubes
Graphene quantum dots
Dye-sensitized solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rational design of the all-carbon and all-scale electrodes
Preparation and characterization of the ternary all-carbon electrodes
Electrocatalytic characteristics of the ternary hybrid
Photovoltaic application of the ternary electrode
Catalytic function of the GQD layer in the ternary hybrid
Functions of the mesoscopic CNT layer in the ternary hybrid
Functions of the CF layer in the ternary hybrid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bio-inspired rational design of high-performance all-carbon and all-scale catalytic electrodes. (a) Schematic diagram of the ternary-hybrid all-carbon electrode composed of wide conductive carbon fibers (1), fine conducting CNTs (2), and highly active ultrafine N-O-GQDs (3), corresponding to counterparts in the body: trunks of blood vessels, capillaries, and functional cells, respectively. (b) Model of GQDs ∼1nm in diameter with N/O edge functionalization (blue sphere N, 10 at%; red sphere O, 16 at%). Charge distribution was calculated and highly charged edge-carbon atoms (1–4) are marked.

Composition and structure of the all-carbon electrodes. (a, b)TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of the colloidal N-O-GQDs (Inset: FFT). (c, d, e) FESEM images of pristine CF (c) and its carbon fibers (d, e). (f, g) FESEM images of the CNT/CF hybrid at low (f) and high (g) magnifications. (h) FESEM image of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CF hybrid. (i) FESEM image of the N-O-GQD/CF hybrid.
Surface chemistry of the ternary hybrid. (a) XPS N1s spectrum. (b) XPS O1s spectrum. (c) FT-IR spectrum. (d) Specific surface area of the CF, CNT/CF, and N-O-GQD/CNT/CF electrodes.

Electrochemical data of the GQD- and Pt-based electrodes. (a) CV curves for the N-O-GQD/CNT/CF and Pt/FTO electrodes; (b) Their Nyquist plots (Inset: equivalent circuit); (c) Their Tafel polarization curves; (d) J–V curves of DSSCs using them as the counter electrode.

Electrochemical data of mesh-type and non-mesh carbon-based electrodes. (a) CV curves for the CF, CNT/CF, N-O-GQD/CF, and N-O-GQD/CNT/CF electrodes; (b) Nyquist plots of mesh electrodes (CF, CNT/CF, and N-O-GQD/CF); (c) Nyquist plot of non-mesh N-O-GQD/ITO electrode. (d) J–V curves of DSSCs from the CF, CNT/CF, N-O-GQD/CF, and N-O-GQD/ITO electrodes.
Photovoltaic parameters from J–V curves and electrochemical parameters from EIS spectra for various electrodes.

head.ce:title: A bionic strategy for addressing scale-span issues in all-carbon electrocatalytic systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Department, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the rational design of an all-carbon and all-scale electrocatalytic system with ultrahigh electrocatalytic activity outperforming Pt-based electrodes by quantum mechanics calculations and bionics. According to the calculations, N and O co-doped graphene quantum dots (N-O-GQDs) could offer an ultrahigh atomic ratio of high-activity edge defects (N-C+-O, O-C+, and N-C+) owing to the most marked edge effect and co-doping effect. To fully activate these atomic-scale defects for catalyzing the two-electron triiodide reduction applied in dye-sensitized solar cells, a mesh-capillary carbon matrix composed of carbon fibers and carbon nanotubes was constructed to well disperse the catalyst and provide fast pathways for both electron transport and electrolyte penetration/diffusion by imitating the sophisticated structure of the blood vessel system. The all-carbon ternary system exhibits high reduction potential (i.e. low overpotential) and low charge-transfer resistance, and delivers high open-circuit voltage of 0.81V, high short circuit current density of 15.77mAcm−2, and high conversion efficiency of 7.68%. Our study helps understand and address general and challenging scale-span issues in electrode reactions for developing more efficient and practical electrocatalytic systems merely by assembling facilely derived carbon materials as the catalyst, matrix or current collector without using other materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Reduction reactions at the cathodes play a crucial role in diverse devices of energy conversion and storage, such as fuel cells [1], metal-air batteries [2], and dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [3–7]. Because of high electrocatalytic activity, noble metals are incorporated in conducting matrices to constitute electrocatalytic systems, such as platinum-loaded carbon for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in fuel cells [1] and platinized F-doped SnO2 (FTO) [3,4] for the triiodide and cobalt reduction in DSSCs. However, the high cost of noble metals has precluded their large-scale commercial applications. Pt-based electrodes also suffer from CO deactivation in fuel cells and corrosion instability in DSSCs. In this context, it is essential to develop high-performance, high-stability, and low-cost electrocatalytic systems to replace noble metal systems for practical applications. Recent studies have suggested that such electrocatalytic systems with these combined advantages could be rationally designed using various carbon materials available as the catalyst or the matrix [8,9,13]. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [1,10–12,17], graphene sheets [13–23], and other carbon forms [24–27] have been shown to serve as a catalyst matrix or electrocatalyst by themselves when doped with electron-accepting heteroatoms (N, O, S). Especially, ultrafine graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the smallest graphene have also exhibited high catalytic activity [28–30] aside from excellent optical/optoelectronic properties [31–34] because of the marked size and edge effects. Albeit these progresses, it has not been reported to construct all-carbon electrodes with electrocatalytic activities beyond that of conventional Pt-based counterparts, without relying on any non-carbon material as the catalyst, the matrix, the binder, or the current collector.

Scale-span issues are universally encountered in electrocatalytic systems, particularly in all-carbon systems, where carbon defects play a crucial role in the catalysis [1,21–23,28–30]. On the one hand, a heterogeneous electrocatalytic reaction always involves electron transport between the macroscopic external circuit and a large number of catalytic units on a molecular or atomic scale. On the other hand, within the electrode system are also involved the macroscopic wetting of electrolyte fluid and the microscopic diffusion of reactive species. All the transport, wetting and diffusion processes are closely correlated with the commonly encountered yet challenging scale-span issues, which exert profound influence on the reaction dynamics. It is thus of fundamental and technical significance to understand and address the scale-span issues in practical non-metal electrocatalytic systems.

In nature, many sophisticated systems have evolved to cope with scale-span issues, such as a body’s blood vessel system. This system takes advantage of hierarchical blood vessels (trunks of arteries/veins and numerous permeable capillaries) to bridge the driving system (the heart) and functional cells for transport and exchange of nutritive and waste substances. Enlightened by the sophisticated scale-span structure in the biological system, we have designed a novel all-carbon and all-scale system composed of three carbon-based hierarchical counterparts to address the scale-span transport and diffusion issues in an electrocatalytic system. This bio-inspired all-carbon system exhibits ultrahigh catalytic activity that outperforms that of Pt-based electrodes.

All calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 program [35]. Geometry optimization and charge-density distributions of N and O co-coped GQDs as a model were carried out using the B3LYP hybrid density functional with 6-31G (d,p) basis sets.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, (Shanghai, China) in analytic purity and directly used without any further purification. The FTO conducting glass, TiO2 paste with average diameter of 20nm, N719 dye, epoxy resin sealant, and the redox electrolyte (OPV-AN-I) were purchased from Yingkou OPV Tech New Energy Co., Ltd, China. A piece of carbon fabric (40cm×40cm) was purchased from PHYCHEMi (Hong Kong) co., Ltd, China.

N-O-GQDs were prepared using the method reported in our previous work [40]. Briefly, pyrene (0.2g) was nitrated into trinitropyrene in hot HNO3 (16mL) at 80°C for 12h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with deionized water until the solution pH close to 7. The resultant yellow 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was dispersed in DI water (120mL) by ultrasonication for 2h. The suspension (40mL) was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (60mL). Hydrazine hydrate (4mL) and ammonium hydroxide (1mL) were dropped into the suspension slowly and stirred for 1h. After mixed, the suspension was heated at 200°C for 12h. Finally, the product containing water-soluble GQDs was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon.

CNT/CF electrodes were fabricated by growing CNTs on CF based on a nickel-catalyzed CVD process [36,37]. In detail, nickel nitrate hexahydrate was firstly dissolved in absolute alcohol and ethylene glycol (1:1). Then, a piece of CF (1.5×2.0cm2) was immersed into the above solution for 1h to adsorb the nickel salt. The treated CF was then put at the center of a tube furnace and a mixed solution (ethanol and ethylene glycol with the volume ratio of 1:5) as the carbon source was placed at the tube entrance. The tube furnace was heated at a rate of 30°C per min to 700°C for 30min under flowing argon atmosphere. After the reaction, the furnace was cooled down to room temperature.

The GQD-based electrodes of N-O-GQD/CF, N-O-GQD/CNT/CF, N-O-GQD/FTO, and N-O-GQD/indium tin oxide-coated polyethylene naphthalate (ITO-PEN) were fabricated by electrophoretic deposition (ED) on substrates of CF, CNT/CF, FTO and ITO-PEN, respectively. Typically, CNT/CF as an anode and a Pt plate as a cathode were immersed into an aqueous solution of N-O-GQDs in a beaker, with distance of ∼2cm between the two electrodes. The deposition was carried out by applying positive voltage (2V) to the anodes. After the deposition, the GQD-loaded N-O-GQD/CF electrode was removed from the beaker, rinsed with deionized water, and annealed at 400°C in argon atmosphere for an hour.

For comparison, Pt/FTO electrodes were prepared by the thermal reduction method. 10mM H2PtCl6·H2O in alcohol was spin-coated on the FTO glass, followed by thermal treatment at 400°C for 30min. Pristine CF electrodes were annealed at 400°C in argon atmosphere for an hour to remove absorbents.

FTO glasses (12Ω square−1) were washed with detergent, DI water, acetone, and ethanol sequentially. In order to prepare the photoanodes for the DSSCs, the TiO2 paste (particle size 20nm) was screen-printed on FTO glass repeatedly until the film thickness reached 12μm. Then, a 4μm-thick light-scattering film (particle size 200nm) was screen-printed on the mesoporousTiO2 film. The films with an area of 0.28cm2 were sintered at 500°C for 30min. When the TiO2 photo anodes were cooled down to 80°C, they were immersed into 0.3mM N719 dye solution in ethyl alcohol for 24h at room temperature. Then, the TiO2 photo anodes were washed with ethyl alcohol and dried in air. The above-prepared electrodes as the counter electrode (CE) were fixed on the photoanodes and electrolyte (OPV-AN-I) was poured on the CEs. Finally, the devices were sealed with epoxy resin sealant.

The morphology of the synthesized samples was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL-200CX) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-6700F, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku 18 KWD/max-2200VPC using Cu Kα radiation. FT-IR spectra of dried samples were recorded with a Bio-Rad FTIR spectrometer FTS165. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The standard methylene blue (MB) dye adsorption method was employed to measure the specific surface areas of carbon materials [38,39].

Current-voltage characteristics of DSSCs were obtained with a computer programmed Keithley 2400 source meter (Keithley, USA) under 1 sun illumination (AM1.5G, 100mWcm−2) by a Zolix's solar simulator. Cyclic voltammo gram (CV) was carried out using a conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell by using CEs as the working electrode, Pt foil as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode in an acetonitrile solution containing 0.1M LiClO4, 10mM LiI, and 1mM I2 at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 using an electrochemical workstation system (Chi660e, Shanghai). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) measurements were conducted in the dark with symmetrical dummy cells (CE/electrolyte/CE) using the workstation system at zero bias potential. The measured frequency ranged from 0.1 to 105 Hz, and the amplitude of the alternating current was set at 10mV. Tafel-polarization measurements were carried out at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 in a symmetrical dummy cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational design of the all-carbon and all-scale electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 a shows the rational design of the high-performance all-carbon and all-scale catalytic electrode using woven carbon fibers in carbon fabric (CF) as the self-supported conducting framework or current collector. On the surface of the carbon fibers, dense and interlinked multiwalled CNTs are coated to form mesoscopic CNT networks within the macroscopic meshes. On the outer surface of the CNTs, a homogeneous catalyst layer is coated from ultrafine GQDs. In the ternary carbon hybrid, the wide conducting carbon fibers are linked to a macroscopic external circuit, like the arteries/veins linked to the heart, the mesoscopic CNTs bridge the fibers and GQDs like blood capillaries bridging arteries/veins and cells, and ultrafine GQDs perform the electrocatalytic function. Aside from the span-scale electron transport pathway (CF→CNTs→GQDs), a large number of span-scale pores within the system are also established to facilitate the wetting and filling of electrolyte solution and the diffusion of reactive species, including macroscopic CF meshes and CNT mesopores.

To maximize their edge-based catalytic activity, the GQDs are doped at edge with N and O heteroatoms (N-O-GQDs). We have established a simple model of N-O-GQDs with edge chemistry configuration shown in Scheme 1b. To understand the N/O co-doping effects on the charge distribution, quantum mechanics calculations were performed using the B3LYP hybrid density functional with 6-31G(d,p) basis sets (Gaussian 09) [35]. As expected, all the edge-carbon atoms adjacent to N or O are highly positively charged (+0.13–0.59 e). As a result of the simultaneous electron-withdrawing effect from adjacent N and O atoms, The edge carbon bonded with both N and O (1, NC+ O) obtains the highest local positive charge (0.59e), much higher than that of active carbon atoms (0.13–0.17 e) in N-doped CNTs [1]. According to the correlation of the catalytic activity of carbon with local positive charge density [1], edge carbons are ordered by the catalytic activity: NC+ O(1)>OC+(2)>NC+(3, 4). This calculation suggests that the N-O-GQDs could have the highest electrocatalytic activity among carbon materials because of the outstanding co-doping effect and the most marked edge effect (For GQDs 1nm in diameter, the ratio of active edge carbon to all carbon is up to 40%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the ternary all-carbon electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The N-O-GQDs were synthesized by a two-step molecular fusion method developed by us [40]. Unlike cutting methods [41–44], the fusion strategy made the GQDs functionalized only at edge sites, whilst the basal plane functionalization was avoided. The GQDs are co-doped with N (amine) and O (hydroxyl) at edge sites with a high concentration of 16.91 at% and 12.53 at%, respectively. Their lateral sizes are controlled in the range of 2–4nm (Fig. 1 a), providing enriched edge active sites for the electrocatalysis. Albeit with the small size, the GQDs keep the ideal single-crystal structure of graphene, as confirmed by the high-resolution TEM and fast Fourier transform (FFT) (Fig. 1b). Apart from high quality, small sizes, and edge functionalization, the large-scale and low-cost synthesis enables their practical applications.

To construct the ternary all-carbon hybrid (N-O-GQD/CNT/CF), a piece of commercial CF was immersed in nickel nitrate solution and treated at 700°C in a tube furnace using ethanol and ethylene glycol as the carbon source. CNTs were grown on the CF to form a binary hybrid (CNT/CF) by a nickel-catalyzed CVD process [36,37]. The CNT/CF hybrid was loaded with the N-O-GQDs to form the ternary hybrid by solution-processable electrophoretic deposition (ED). The as-prepared all-carbon electrode was annealed at 400°C to keep the good electrical contact of the GQDs with the CNT/CF matrix.

The surface structures of the CF and its hybrids, CNT/CF and N-O-GQD/CNT/CF, were imaged using FE-SEM. The commercial CF is woven with carbon fibers 10–12μm in diameter (Fig. 1c). There are large gaps or meshes between carbon fibers (Fig. 1d), whose surface is smooth (Fig. 1e), but after the CNT coating, the fibers are roughened (Fig. 1f). Magnification view shows that a mesoporous CNT network forms from dense, interlinked CNTs with diameters of 17–27nm on the CF surface (Fig. 1g). Ni particles were observed on the tube end, suggesting the Ni catalyzed growth of the CNTs (Fig. S1). After GQD coating on the CNT/CF, the CNTs become ‘fatter’ owing to the formation of a thin carbon shell layer (∼10–15nm thick), while the mesoporous CNT network is kept (Fig. 1h).

The N-O-GQD coating is also confirmed by the EDS analysis and mapping (Fig. S2). Besides C, N, and O, remaining Ni is also detected in the hybrid. For the N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 a), the peaks at 394.2, 399.2 and 404.9eV are assigned to amino N, pyridinic N and NO2, respectively. For O 1s (Fig. 2b), signals of OH and −NO2 groups are observed at 531.9 and 536.9eV, respectively. The result indicates that N and O are co-doped largely as pyridinic N and −OH. The FT-IR spectrum shows NH vibrations at 3200 and 1620cm−1, NC vibration at 1317cm−1, O-H vibration at 3400cm−1, and C-OH vibration at 1270cm−1 (Fig. 2c). The effect of the surface modification on enhancing the specific surface area (SSA) of the CF was observed. By the CNT modification, SSA is increased from 146 to 856m2 g−1. With further GQD coating, there is only a negligible decrease in SSA (Fig. 2d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic characteristics of the ternary hybrid
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrocatalytic activity of the NOGQDs in the CNT/CF matrix was evaluated based on the reduction reaction (1) in the I3 −/I− redox couple most studied in DSSCs.
(1) I3 − +2e− ↔3I−
(2) 2I3 − ↔3I2 +2e−
The reduction of I3 − to I− is dynamically more unfavorable compared with the reduction of Co3+ to Co2+, because the former is a two-electron process [45] while the latter is just a one-electron process. Moreover, the reduction of I3 − needs additional energy to cleave the three covalent bonds within I3 − ions. Therefore, graphene materials used as counter electrodes in DSSCs perform worse in iodide-based electrolyte than cobalt-based electrolyte [21–23], owing to the higher overpotential. This limitation could be overcome by the N-O-GQD electrocatalyst, which can offer high-activity edge defects at a high concentration. The highly charged edge defects such as NC+ O, OC+, and NC+ could enhance the absorption of I3 − ions and facilitate the cleavage of the covalent bonds, leading to significant decrease in the overpotential.

To verify the hypothesis, we performed cyclic voltametry (CV) measurements on the N-O-GQD/CNT/CF electrode in a three-electrode setup, where Pt foil was used as a counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, and iodide solution in acetonitrile as the electrolyte. An electrode of Pt/FTO was used as the reference. Fig. 3 a shows the CV curves for the GQD- and Pt-based electrodes over the potential range of −0.5 to +0.5V (vs Ag/AgCl). For the GQD electrode, there is a couple of exceptionally strong oxidation/reduction peaks (AOX/ARE) at +0.295 and +0.03V, corresponding to the reactions of Eqs. (2) and (1), respectively. For the conventional Pt/FTO electrode, the corresponding AOX and ARE peaks are positioned at +0.30V and −0.124V, respectively. Compared with the Pt electrode, the GQD electrode shows a higher reduction potential VRE (i.e. lower overpotential). Moreover, the reduction peak current density (JRE) is 2.6 times higher that of the Pt electrode. Also, there is a much lower peak-to-peak voltage separation (ΔEP) (0.265V for the GQDs, 0.424V for the Pt). All the three CV data (VRE, ΔEP, and JRE) consistently point to the conclusion that the N-O-GQDs have superb electrocatalytic activity outperforming that of the Pt electrodes. To our best knowledge, the ΔEP value for the GQD electrode reported here is the smallest among Pt-, carbon-, and compound-based materials reported so far for the triiodide reduction [20,46–49].

Aside from the CV data, other electrochemical data based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Tafel polarization also support the conclusion. Fig. 3b shows the Nyquist plots of the GQD and Pt electrodes. By fitting the Nyquist plots with the equivalent circuit diagram (inset), charge-transfer resistance (Rct ) and series resistance (Rs ) were drawn and listed in Table 1 . The GQD electrode has lower Rct (0.61Ω cm2) and Rs (2.76Ωcm2) than those of the Pt electrode (Rct , 0.98Ωcm2; Rs , 4.81Ω cm2). The Tafel polarization curves of the GQD and Pt electrodes also show the higher electrocatalytic activity in the GQD sample (Fig. 3c). From the Tafel curves, a higher exchange current density (J0 ) can be obtained in the GQD electrode. The higher J0 means the lower Rct , because J0 is inversely proportional to Rct . The EIS and Tafel data suggest a faster reaction dynamics at the GQD electrode/electrolyte interface for the reduction of I3 − to I−.

The cycling stability of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CF ternary hybrid electrode was also explored. Fig. S3 shows its cyclic voltammograms for 100 cycles. After 100 cycles, the reduction peak current density (JRE) is reduced only by 8%, while the reduction peak shifts to more positive potential. This result indicates that the GQD electrode exhibits a high cyclic stability in the corrosive electrolyte besides the ultrahigh electrocatalytic activity for the reduction of I3 −.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic application of the ternary electrode
ce:section.ce:para: As an example, the high-activity GQD system was applied as an all-carbon counter electrode in iodine-based DSSCs. Fig. 3d shows the J–V curves of DSSCs from the GQD and Pt systems. The photovoltaic parameters derived from the J–V curves under 1 sun illumination (AM1.5G, 100mWcm−2) include open-circuit voltage (Voc ), short circuit current density (Jsc ), fill factor (FF), and conversion efficiency (η) of the cells (Table 1). Without optimizing the photoanode, the Pt-based DSSC exhibits Voc of 0.76V, Jsc of 14.17mAcm−2, FF of 63%, and η of 6.90%. Under the identical conditions, however, the replace of the noble metal electrode with the all-carbon electrode can improve three photovoltaic parameters of Voc (0.81V), Jsc (15.77mAcm−2), and η (7.68%). The ternary all-carbon hybrid has created the highest conversion efficiency among all-carbon counter electrodes reported so far [17,18]. For example, Ogale et al. used pyrolytic carbon cloth as the counter electrode of a DSSC with photovoltaic parameters of Voc (0.73V), Jsc (11.6mAcm−2), FF (65%), and η (5.8%) [17]. Cheng et al. prepared vertically aligned CNTs on graphene paper as the counter electrode of a DSSC with conversion efficiency of 6.05% [18]. Compared with these all-carbon electrodes, the GQD-based all-carbon electrode delivers much higher short circuit current density and open-circuit voltage, which are correlated with the active sites of the GQDs with a much higher concentration and an ultralow overpotential or ultrahigh reduction potential, respectively. The Voc value of a DSSC is determined by the difference between the flatband potential of the semiconductor photoanode and the formal potential of the I3 −/I− couple. As demonstrated by the CV data (Fig. 3a), the formal potential of the I3 −/I− couple on the GQD electrode shifts more positively than that on the Pt electrode by a difference of 154mV. Therefore, the high-activity GQDs as the DSSC’s catalyst can overcome the limitation of the lower redox potential of the I3 −/I− electrolyte than the Co3+/Co2+ couple to a certain extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic function of the GQD layer in the ternary hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: It is understood under the scale-span frame what roles the three components (N-O-GQDs, CF, and CNTs) of the ternary hybrid play on the overall electrochemical performance of the all-carbon electrode. The first question is whether the reduction reaction takes place on the N-O-GQD layer. To answer it, we compare the CV data from the ternary, binary, and unitary all-carbon electrodes in Fig. 4 a. It is noted that without the GQD coating, the well confined reduction peak at 0.03V observed in the ternary hybrid disappears in the CF and CNT/CF electrodes. Instead, a weak reduction terrace at lower potentials was observed. This marked contrast clearly shows that the high-activity sites indeed originate in the GQD layer rather than the underneath CNT or carbon fiber layers. This contrast also shows that the remaining Ni metal within the CNT layer contributes little to the overall electrocatalytic performance in the ternary system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functions of the mesoscopic CNT layer in the ternary hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the important role of the CNT layer on the electrocatalytic performance of the ternary system, the GQDs were directly deposited on the carbon fabric to form the N-O-GQD/CF hybrid. The surface imaging of the N-O-GQD/CF hybrid shows that the N-O-GQDs were found to aggregate into large irregular carbon particles in various sizes (Fig. 1i), rather than form a uniform GQD coating on the CNTs in the ternary system (Fig. 1h). This contrast indicates that the CNTs loaded on the CF substrate as a mesoporous CNT network can serve as a buffer layer to better disperse the GQDs within the ternary system. Compared with the ternary system, the binary system exhibits a lower catalytic activity owing to the lower dispersion of the GQDs, as confirmed by the CV curve (Fig. 4a), where no marked reduction peak was observed. Aside from the dispersion ability, the highly conductive CNT mesoporous layer also serves as an indispensable linkage to bridge the macroscopic external circuit (carbon fibers also included) with a large number of highly active sites in the GQD layer, thus forming the highly efficient, scale-span electron transport system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functions of the CF layer in the ternary hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: We then assess the functions of the third component CF in the ternary system. Structurally, the CF serves as a self-supported conducting framework for the whole system. Moreover, the CF provides macroscopic large meshes well fused with the mesoscopic pores within the CNT layer to form a more permeable, hierarchical porous network. This scale-spanning porous network can overcome the limitations of the macroscopic electrolyte penetration and microscopic diffusion of reaction species within electrodes to the reaction kinetics at the solid-liquid interface. To observe the effects of the hierarchical porous network on the catalytic kinetics, the charge-transfer resistance Rct for a series of mesh-type carbon electrodes, such as the pristine CF, CNT/CF and N-O-GQD/CF as well as N-O-GQD/CNT/CF, was measured and analyzed from the Nyquist plots (Fig. 4b), because Rct is the most sensitive to the electrode reaction kinetics, i.e. Rct is inversely proportional to the rate of the reduction of I3 − to I−. Before the measurements, the pristine CF was treated at 400°C in argon atmosphere to increase the surface wetting ability by removing hydrophobic absorbents [22]. From Table 1, all the mesh electrodes show extremely low Rct values from 0.61 to 1.62Ωcm2 compared with the reported carbon-based non-mesh electrodes, such as graphene films on FTO (8.5–38.0Ωcm2) [21] and mesoporous carbons (10.1–30Ωcm2) [50]. Even for the pristine CF without any modification, Rct is as low as 1.62Ωcm2. A lower Rct (0.74Ωcm2) was also observed in pyrolytic carbon cloth with a similar mesh structure, albeit with higher series resistance (18.7Ωcm2) [17]. For comparison, non-mesh N-O-GQD-based electrodes on ITO substrates were prepared using the ED method. N and O co-doped carbon nanoparticles with sizes of 20–40nm were deposited on the non-mesh substrates (Fig. S4). Compared with the Nyquist plots of all the mesh electrodes (Fig. 4b), the Nyquist plot of the non-mesh GQD electrodes shows a larger semicircle at high frequencies, corresponding to a higher Rct of 17.6Ωcm2 (Fig. 4c), suggesting a lower reaction dynamics in the non-mesh carbon electrodes. These results highlight the importance of the introduction of reasonably large meshes or similar open structures (e.g. vertically aligned CNTs [1] and CNT films [10]) within carbon-based electrodes, because the open structure plays a crucial role in facilitating the electrode reaction kinetics. As evidenced by the much lower Rct (0.61Ωcm2) and Rs (2.76Ωcm2) in the ternary all-carbon electrode composed of the macroscopic CF meshes and the CNT mesopores, the scale-span combination of a macroscopic open structure with a mesoscopic pore structure can provide a more efficient wetting and diffusion pathway for addressing the second scale-span issue that involves the limited reaction dynamics related to the electrolyte wetting and diffusion of reactive species within carbon-based electrodes. Besides the low charge-transfer resistance, the ‘mesh-wetting’ effect also leads to higher FF (60–65%) in the mesh-type all-carbon electrodes (Table 1). In contrast, for the non-mesh GQD electrode, the FF value is only 40%. Fig. 4d shows the J–V curves of DSSCs from the mesh-type carbon-based electrodes with higher current density kept in the wider voltage range than in the GQD/ITO electrode, therefore yielding higher conversion efficiencies of 6.07–7.68%.

Finally, we need explain more clearly the bionic design principle of the unique GQDs/CNT/CF system on how to address scale-span issues involved in the catalytic activity and reaction dynamics. Firstly, the GQDs/CNT/CF system is to mimic a body’s blood vessel system, which is composed of scale-pan vessel parts, including trunks of arteries/veins and numerous permeable capillaries to bridge the driving system (the heart) with functional cells for facilitating the transport of nutritive and waste substances. Like the vessel system, electrocatalytic electrode systems also involve fast transport from macroscopic to microscopic scales, though the transported species (both electrons and electrolyte molecules/ions) in the latter are different from those of the former. In this all-carbon electrocatalytic system, the most crucial component is the highly edge-active, N and O co-doped graphene quantum dots as the molecular electrocatalyst. To make the catalytic carbon defects more effective, the electrocatalyst on molecular or atomic scale needs a mesoscopic linkage with a macroscopic external circuit to construct fast electron transport pathway. In this way, the CF-supported capillary-like CNTs to disperse the molecular catalyst on their surface serve a necessary linkage that plays a role like that of the capillaries in the blood vessel system. On the other hand, the blood vessel system forms a sophisticated network throughout the whole body. Similarly, the macroscopic meshes from the CF and the mesoscopic pores from the CNTs also constitute a highly permeable, hierarchical porous network within the all-carbon electrode to facilitate the macroscopic electrolyte penetration and microscopic diffusion of reaction species within electrodes to enhance the reaction kinetics at the solid-liquid interface. Therefore, we believe that our bionic design of all-carbon catalytic electrodes can provide insight for the design of other electrode systems, which are also involved scale-span issues of electron transport and electrolyte penetration.

In conclusion, we have for the first time attempted to simultaneously address the scale-span issues of electron transport and electrolyte penetration/diffusion in all-carbon electrocatalytic systems by simulating the unique scale-span structure of the blood vessel system that combines the transport and penetration functions. We found that the scale-spanning porous network that fuses macroscopic meshes with mesoscopic pores in the CNT/CF matrix can overcome the limitations of the macroscopic electrolyte penetration and microscopic diffusion of reaction species within electrodes to the reaction kinetics at the solid-liquid interface. By inducing a large amount of carbon meshes in the all-carbon electrodes, the charge-transfer resistance can be reduced significantly compared with the non-mesh electrodes. We have also designed ultrahigh-activity GQDs with enriched edge-active sites in the all-carbon electrodes by inducing the edge effect and the N and O co-doping effect on the catalysis. To fully activate these highly charged edge defects such as NC+ O, OC+, and NC+, a blood capillary-like carbon network of CNT/CF was used as the permeable, conductive mesoscopic matrix to bridge the active defects with the external circuit and liquid electrolyte. The all-carbon GQD system composed of doped GQDs, CNTs and carbon fibers exhibits an exceptionally high catalytic activity outperforming that of Pt-based electrodes, including ultralow reduction overpotential and low charge-transfer resistance for catalyzing the triiodide reduction. This low-cost all-carbon electrode has been employed as the wearable counter electrode in iodine-based DSSCs, which deliver high open-circuit voltage of 0.81V, high short circuit current density of 15.77mAcm−2, acceptable fill factor of 60%, and high conversion efficiency of 7.68%. The all-carbon span-scale hybrid in replace of noble metals would be applied in diverse practical devices of energy conversion and storage, such as fuel cells, metal-air batteries, and dye-sensitized solar cells. Especially, our bionic strategy provides insight into the scale-span structural design of various electrocatalytic systems for addressing scale-span issues.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 91233102, 21471098), the Shanghai Sailing Program (No. 16YF1404400), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 16ZR1412100), and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.05.124.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
